firmware.name: grblHAL
firmware.description: A 32-bit port and extension of grbl. Adds support for more axes (up to 9), tool changes, canned cycles, macros, networking, spindle sync, and plugin architecture. Maintains backwards compatibility with grbl.

codes.name: Rapid Move
codes.description: Moves the tool to the specified coordinates at the maximum traverse rate. Each axis moves at its own maximum rate. Supports up to 9 axes (XYZABCUVW) depending on driver configuration.

examples.description: Rapid move to origin at Z safe height
Rapid rotate A axis to 90 degrees

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. grblHAL adds support for up to 9 axes (A, B, C, U, V, W) depending on the driver.
Same behavior. Both support up to 9 axes. LinuxCNC supports path blending with G64; grblHAL added G64 support as a compile-time option.
Smoothieware treats G0 identically with separately configurable per-axis max rates.
In RepRapFirmware, G0 and G1 are identical and both use F. In grblHAL, G0 always moves at max rate regardless of F.

modeNotes.description: Same as grbl: G0 moves automatically disable the laser (S value preserved but PWM output is 0).

codes.notes: Same as grbl but with extended axis support. Supports up to 9 axes (XYZABCUVW) depending on the driver/board configuration, versus grbl's XYZ only.

codes.name: Linear Move
codes.description: Controlled straight-line cutting move at the specified feed rate. All axes arrive simultaneously. Supports up to 9 axes depending on driver configuration.

parameters.name: F
parameters.description: Feed rate in units/min (or units/rev with G95)

examples.description: Plunge cut to Z-1 at 100 units/min

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. grblHAL adds multi-axis support and feed-per-revolution mode (G95).
Same behavior. Both support additional axes and G95 feed mode.
Same behavior.
RepRapFirmware uses S parameter on G1 for laser power. grblHAL uses S on M3/M4.

modeNotes.description: Same dynamic power scaling as grbl when used with M4. Inline S value changes update PWM without stopping motion.

codes.notes: Same as grbl but with extended axis support. Feed rate (F) is modal and persists until changed. Can use G95 feed-per-revolution mode (new in grblHAL).

codes.name: Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Clockwise circular or helical interpolation in the currently selected plane (G17/G18/G19). Internally rendered as tiny line segments controlled by arc tolerance setting.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Arc center offset in X (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Y (incremental from current position)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Z (incremental from current position)

examples.description: CW arc to X10 Y10 with center offset I5 J0
CW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5 (shorter arc)

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical arc behavior and IJK interpretation.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC also supports absolute IJK mode (G90.1); grblHAL only supports incremental IJK (G91.1).
Same behavior.
Same behavior.

modeNotes.description: Laser is active during arcs. With M4, dynamic power scales with instantaneous speed.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Only incremental IJK mode (G91.1) is supported. Use either IJK offsets or R radius, not both.

codes.name: Counter-Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Counter-clockwise circular or helical interpolation. Same as G2 but in the opposite direction.

examples.description: CCW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl.

codes.name: Dwell
codes.description: Pauses execution for a specified duration. Non-modal.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
LinuxCNC uses P for seconds. Some controllers interpret P as milliseconds; grblHAL uses seconds like grbl.

modeNotes.description: With M3 (constant), laser stays on during dwell. With M4 (dynamic), laser turns off during dwell since speed is zero.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. P is in seconds (not milliseconds).

codes.name: Cubic Spline
codes.description: Cubic spline interpolation in the current plane. Uses control points to define a smooth curve. New in grblHAL, not available in grbl.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: X offset of first control point from start

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Y offset of first control point from start

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: X offset of second control point from end

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Y offset of second control point from end

examples.description: Cubic spline to X10 Y10 with control point offsets

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. Not available in grbl.
LinuxCNC supports G5 (cubic) and G5.1 (quadratic) splines with the same syntax.
Smoothieware does not support spline interpolation.
RepRapFirmware does not support spline interpolation natively.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Cubic B-spline interpolation for smooth curves without arc approximation.

codes.name: Quadratic Spline
codes.description: Quadratic B-spline interpolation using a single control point. New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Quadratic spline to X10 Y10 with control point offset

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. Not available in grbl.
LinuxCNC supports G5.1 quadratic splines with the same syntax.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Quadratic B-spline for smooth curves with a single control point.

codes.name: Lathe Diameter Mode
codes.description: Interprets X axis values as diameters in lathe mode. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have lathe mode.
Same behavior. Standard lathe diameter mode.
Smoothieware does not support lathe diameter mode.
RepRapFirmware does not support lathe modes.

modeNotes.description: Only available when lathe mode is enabled in the driver configuration.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Only meaningful in lathe mode. X values represent diameters, so X20 means the tool is at radius 10.

codes.name: Lathe Radius Mode
codes.description: Interprets X axis values as radii in lathe mode. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have lathe mode.
Same behavior. Standard lathe radius mode.

modeNotes.description: Only available when lathe mode is enabled.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Only meaningful in lathe mode. X values represent actual radii.

codes.name: Set Work Coordinate Offset (Absolute)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset to absolute values. Stored persistently in non-volatile memory.

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y20 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 offset to X10 Y20 Z0

examples.code: G10 L2 P7 X0 Y0 Z0

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. grblHAL extends P range to include P7-P9 for G59.1-G59.3.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC supports the same P range.

codes.notes: Same as grbl with extended coordinate system support (P7-P9 for G59.1-G59.3). Persistent in non-volatile memory.

codes.name: Set Work Coordinate Offset (Relative)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset such that the current position becomes the specified coordinate value. Persistent in non-volatile memory.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: Desired X position at current location

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Desired Y position at current location

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Desired Z position at current location

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position reads as X0 Y0 Z0

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Extended to support P7-P9 for G59.1-G59.3.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Commonly used for setting work zero at the current position.

codes.name: Set Tool Table (Computed)
codes.description: Sets tool table entry with computed offsets. The offset is calculated from the current position relative to the active coordinate system. New in grblHAL, driver dependent.

examples.description: Set tool 1 Z offset and radius

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have a tool table.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC supports additional parameters (frontangle, backangle, orientation).

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Driver dependent: requires a driver/plugin that supports the tool table. Used with M6 tool changes and G43 tool length offsets.

codes.name: Set Tool Table (Relative, WCS)
codes.description: Sets tool table entry such that the current position with the current offsets becomes the specified value. New in grblHAL, driver dependent.

examples.description: Set tool 1 offset so current Z reads as 0 in WCS

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have a tool table.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Calculates tool offset based on current position relative to the work coordinate system.

codes.name: Set Tool Table (Relative, Machine)
codes.description: Sets tool table entry relative to the current machine position (G53). New in grblHAL, driver dependent.

examples.description: Set tool 1 offset so current Z reads as 0 in machine coordinates

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have a tool table.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Calculates tool offset based on current position relative to machine coordinates.

codes.name: XY Plane Select
codes.description: Selects XY as the active plane for arc commands (G2/G3). I and J specify arc center offsets. Default plane.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Default plane selection.

codes.name: ZX Plane Select
codes.description: Selects ZX (XZ) as the active plane for arc commands. I and K specify arc center offsets.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.name: YZ Plane Select
codes.description: Selects YZ as the active plane for arc commands. J and K specify arc center offsets.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl.

codes.name: Inch Mode
codes.description: Sets units to inches for all subsequent commands.

examples.description: Set inch mode, move to 1 inch in X and Y

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.name: Millimeter Mode
codes.description: Sets units to millimeters for all subsequent commands.

examples.description: Set mm mode, move to 25.4mm in X and Y

Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl.

codes.description: Moves through an optional intermediate point, then to the predefined position stored in non-volatile memory. This is NOT homing.

examples.description: Move Z to 0, then go to stored position 1

Same behavior.
WARNING: Smoothieware uses G28 for homing, not for moving to a stored position.
WARNING: RepRapFirmware uses G28 for homing. Different behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Use $H for homing, G28 for moving to a stored position.

codes.description: Stores the current position as the predefined position 1 (used by G28). Persistent in non-volatile memory.

examples.description: Save current position as predefined position 1

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl.

codes.name: Go to Predefined Position 2
codes.description: Moves through an optional intermediate point, then to the second predefined position stored in non-volatile memory.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Identical behavior.

examples.description: Save current position as predefined position 2

Same behavior.

codes.name: Spindle-Synchronized Motion (Threading)
codes.description: Moves the Z axis synchronized to spindle rotation for single-point threading on a lathe. Requires a spindle encoder/index pulse. New in grblHAL.

Same behavior. Both require spindle encoder feedback.
Smoothieware does not support spindle-synchronized motion.
RepRapFirmware does not support threading.

modeNotes.description: Requires lathe mode and spindle encoder support in the driver.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Requires spindle encoder/index pulse hardware and driver support. Used for single-point threading on lathes.

codes.name: Rigid Tapping
codes.description: Spindle-synchronized tapping cycle. The spindle reverses at the bottom of the hole to retract the tap. Requires spindle encoder. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support rigid tapping.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Requires spindle encoder support. The spindle automatically reverses at the bottom to retract.

codes.description: Straight probe toward workpiece. Errors if probe does not make contact.

examples.description: Probe downward at 50 units/min, error if no contact

Same behavior.

Same behavior.

codes.description: Straight probe away from workpiece. Does NOT error if probe fails to break contact.

examples.description: Probe upward away from surface, no error if no break

Same behavior.

codes.name: Cancel Cutter Radius Compensation
codes.description: Cancels cutter radius compensation. grblHAL accepts this command for compatibility but does not implement cutter compensation.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Both accept G40 but do not implement cutter compensation.
LinuxCNC fully implements cutter compensation (G41/G42). grblHAL only accepts G40 for compatibility.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Accepted for compatibility only. Cutter compensation (G41/G42) is not implemented.

codes.name: Tool Length Offset (From Tool Table)
codes.description: Applies the tool length offset from the tool table for the specified tool number. New in grblHAL.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Tool number to load offset from (if omitted, uses current tool)

examples.description: Apply tool length offset for tool 1 from tool table
Change to tool 1 and apply its offset

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not have a tool table or G43.
Same behavior. Both load offset from tool table.
Smoothieware does not support tool length offsets from a tool table.
RepRapFirmware handles tool offsets differently via G10 Pn.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Loads tool length offset from the tool table. Requires tool table support in the driver. Use G10 L1/L10/L11 to set tool table entries.

codes.name: Dynamic Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Applies an explicit tool length offset specified directly in the command, without referencing the tool table. Supported in both grbl and grblHAL.

examples.description: Apply a Z tool length offset of -10.5

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Both support dynamic tool length offset.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Allows setting tool length offset without a tool table.

codes.name: Apply Additional Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Adds an additional tool length offset on top of the current offset (stacking). New in grblHAL.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Tool number to add offset from

examples.description: Apply tool 1 offset, then add tool 2 offset on top

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support offset stacking.
Same behavior. Both allow stacking additional offsets.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Allows stacking tool length offsets for multi-offset setups.

codes.name: Cancel Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Cancels any active tool length offset (G43, G43.1, G43.2).

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Both support G49 to cancel tool length offsets.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl.

codes.name: Cancel Scaling
codes.description: Cancels any active scaling factors set by G51. Resets all axis scale factors to 1.0. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support scaling.
LinuxCNC does not have an equivalent scaling command.
Smoothieware does not support coordinate scaling.
RepRapFirmware does not support coordinate scaling.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Unique to grblHAL among the common CNC firmwares. Cancels G51 scaling.

codes.name: Set Scaling Factors
codes.description: Sets per-axis scaling factors. All subsequent coordinate values are multiplied by the scale factor. New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Scale X and Y coordinates by 2x (double size)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support scaling.
LinuxCNC does not have a direct equivalent. Scaling is typically done in CAM.
Smoothieware does not support coordinate scaling.
RepRapFirmware does not support coordinate scaling.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Unique feature for on-the-fly coordinate scaling. Useful for resizing parts without re-generating G-code.

codes.name: Move in Machine Coordinates
codes.description: Temporarily overrides the active work coordinate system and moves in machine (absolute) coordinates. Non-modal, only applies to the line it appears on.

examples.description: Rapid to machine Z zero (typically top of travel)

Same behavior.

Same behavior. LinuxCNC supports G59.1-G59.3.

codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 8. New in grblHAL.

codes.name: Exact Path Mode
codes.description: Machine stops at each programmed point to achieve exact path. Default mode in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: Same as grbl. Default path control mode.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: Same as grbl. Default path control mode.

codes.name: Exact Stop Mode
codes.description: Machine decelerates to a complete stop at each programmed point. More precise than G61. New in grblHAL, compile-time option.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not distinguish between G61 and G61.1.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Compile-time option. Decelerates to zero velocity at each endpoint.

codes.name: Path Blending Mode
codes.description: Enables path blending (continuous mode) where the machine smoothly blends between segments without coming to a full stop. New in grblHAL, compile-time option.

examples.description: Enable path blending with default tolerance

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support path blending.
Same behavior. LinuxCNC also supports P and Q parameters for tolerance control.
Smoothieware has a different junction deviation approach, not G64.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Compile-time option. Bridges the gap between grbl (no path blending) and LinuxCNC (full path blending). Significantly improves surface finish on curved surfaces.

codes.name: Macro Call
codes.description: Calls a macro subroutine by number with optional arguments. New in grblHAL, unique feature.

parameters.name: A-Z
parameters.description: Arguments passed to the macro (mapped to local variables)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support macros.
LinuxCNC uses O-word subroutines (O100 call) instead of G65. Different syntax, similar concept.
Smoothieware does not support macro calls.
RepRapFirmware uses M98 for macro/subroutine calls.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Fanuc-style macro calling. Requires macro plugin. Arguments A-Z are mapped to local variables #1-#26.

codes.name: Modal Macro Call
codes.description: Like G65 but modal: the macro is called automatically after every subsequent motion block until cancelled with G67. New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Macro 100 runs after each G1 move until G67 cancels it

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support macros.
LinuxCNC does not have a direct G66 equivalent.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Modal macro call. Requires macro plugin.

codes.name: Cancel Modal Macro Call
codes.description: Cancels the modal macro call set by G66. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support macros.
LinuxCNC does not have a direct G67 equivalent.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Cancels G66 modal macro.

codes.name: Peck Drilling Cycle (Chip Break)
codes.description: High-speed peck drilling cycle. Drills in increments with a small retract to break chips, then returns to peck depth. Faster than G83 because it does not fully retract between pecks. New in grblHAL.

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Retract plane (rapid to this height before drilling)

examples.description: Peck drill at (10,10) to Z-20 with 5mm pecks and chip-break retract

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support canned cycles.
Same behavior. Standard canned drilling cycle.
Smoothieware does not support canned cycles.
RepRapFirmware does not support canned drilling cycles.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Canned drilling cycle. The chip-break retract is a small distance (typically controlled by a parameter), unlike G83 which fully retracts to R plane.

codes.name: Threading Cycle
codes.description: Multi-pass threading cycle for lathes. Automatically calculates passes with decreasing depth of cut. Requires spindle encoder. New in grblHAL.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Thread taper (difference in radius over length)

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Compound slide angle (29.5 for unified, 30 for metric)

examples.description: Thread M12x1.5 for 20mm, 0.3mm initial cut, 0.92mm total depth

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support threading.
Same behavior. Both implement multi-pass threading with similar parameters.
Smoothieware does not support threading.
RepRapFirmware does not support threading.

modeNotes.description: Only available in lathe mode with spindle encoder support.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Multi-pass threading cycle for lathes. Requires spindle encoder hardware and driver support. Modeled after LinuxCNC G76.

codes.name: Cancel Canned Cycle
codes.description: Cancels any active canned cycle (G73, G81-G89). New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support canned cycles.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Required to cancel modal canned cycles before switching to other motion modes.

codes.name: Drilling Cycle

examples.description: Drill at (10,10) to Z-15, retract to R2

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support canned cycles.
Same behavior. Standard canned drilling cycle.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Simplest canned drilling cycle. Modal: subsequent XY positions will repeat the cycle.

codes.name: Drilling Cycle with Dwell
codes.description: Drilling cycle with a dwell (pause) at the bottom of the hole. Useful for spot drilling or ensuring a clean hole bottom. New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Drill with 0.5 second dwell at bottom

Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Like G81 but with a dwell at the bottom.

codes.name: Peck Drilling Cycle (Full Retract)
codes.description: Deep hole peck drilling cycle. Drills in increments, fully retracting to R plane between each peck for chip clearing. Slower than G73 but better for deep holes. New in grblHAL.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support canned cycles.
Same behavior.

codes.name: Right-Hand Tapping Cycle
codes.description: Tapping cycle for right-hand (standard) threads. Feeds down at calculated rate based on pitch, then reverses to retract. New in grblHAL.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Canned tapping cycle.

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Feed Out)
codes.description: Boring cycle that feeds to depth and feeds back out at the same rate. No dwell at bottom. New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Bore to Z-20 and feed out

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support canned cycles.
Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Boring cycle with feed retract for good surface finish.

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Spindle Stop, Rapid Out)

Same behavior.

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Dwell, Feed Out)
codes.description: Boring cycle with dwell at bottom before feeding out. Combines features of G82 (dwell) and G85 (feed retract). New in grblHAL.

examples.description: Bore to Z-20, dwell 1 second, feed out

Same behavior.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Boring cycle with dwell for best surface finish at bottom of bore.

crossReferences.note: Modified from grbl. In grbl, $22 is a simple 0/1 boolean. In grblHAL, it is a bitmask: bit 0 = enable homing, bit 1 = enable single-axis homing commands ($HX etc.), bit 2 = force homing on startup, bit 3 = manual homing via limit switches, bit 4 = override locks.

codes.notes: Modified from grbl. Default: 0. Bitmask bits: 0=enable homing, 1=enable single-axis commands ($HX/$HY/$HZ), 2=force homing on init (alarm until homed), 3=manual homing (set home at current position when limit switch triggered), 4=override locks. Common values: 1 (basic like grbl), 3 (with single-axis), 5 (with forced startup homing), 7 (all three).

codes.name: Mode of Operation
codes.description: Sets the machine mode of operation. Extended from grbl to add lathe mode. In normal mode (0), spindle operates as a standard CNC spindle. In laser mode (1), the PWM output is linked to motion for safe laser operation. In lathe mode (2), enables CSS (constant surface speed) and feed-per-revolution modes.

crossReferences.note: Extended from grbl. grbl only supports 0 (normal) and 1 (laser). grblHAL adds 2 (lathe) for CSS and feed-per-revolution support.

modeNotes.description: Same as grbl: G0 disables laser, M4 enables dynamic power scaling.
Enables G95 (feed per revolution), G96/G97 (constant surface speed), and diameter/radius programming modes. Requires spindle encoder ($38).

codes.notes: Modified from grbl. Default: 0. Lathe mode (2) is new in grblHAL and requires a spindle encoder ($38 for PPR configuration). Lathe mode enables G95, G96, G97, and other lathe-specific G-codes.

codes.name: Spindle PWM Frequency
codes.description: Sets the PWM frequency for the spindle output in Hz. Different spindle types and VFDs may require specific PWM frequencies for proper operation. Too low causes audible whine; too high may not be supported by the driver circuit.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. In grbl, PWM frequency is fixed at compile time (~1kHz on AVR).

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Allows runtime configuration of PWM frequency. grbl has this hard-coded. Common values: 1000Hz for RC ESC spindles, 5000-10000Hz for VFDs with 0-10V input, 20000Hz+ for ultrasonic (inaudible) operation.

codes.name: Stepper Deenergize Mask
codes.description: Per-axis bitmask controlling which stepper motors are deenergized (disabled) when the idle timeout ($1) expires. Allows keeping specific axes energized while others power down. Essential for machines with a vertical Z axis that drops under gravity.

examples.description: Keep Z energized, deenergize X and Y
Keep all XYZ energized (similar to $1=255 but works with timeout)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl only has $1 (global idle delay) with no per-axis control. In grbl, the only option for keeping Z energized is $1=255 which keeps ALL axes energized.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 0. Bitmask: X=1, Y=2, Z=4, A=8, etc. A set bit means that axis stays energized after idle timeout. Solves the common grbl problem of needing to keep Z energized (to prevent gravity drop) while allowing X/Y to deenergize (to reduce heat/power).

codes.name: Spindle PPR
codes.description: Sets the spindle encoder pulses per revolution (PPR). Required for spindle synchronized motion (threading), CSS (constant surface speed), and feed-per-revolution mode. Set to 0 if no encoder is installed.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support spindle encoders.
LinuxCNC configures spindle encoder in HAL files.

modeNotes.description: Required for lathe operations including threading (G33/G76), CSS (G96), and feed-per-revolution (G95).

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 0 (no encoder). Must match your physical encoder's PPR. Required for spindle sync features: threading (G33, G76), rigid tapping (G33.1), CSS (G96), and feed-per-revolution (G95). Higher PPR gives better resolution for threading.

codes.name: Homing Locate Cycles
codes.description: Sets the number of locate (slow approach) cycles performed during homing. After the initial fast seek, the axis pulls off and re-approaches this many times at the slow locate rate ($24) for improved accuracy.

examples.description: One locate cycle (default, same as grbl)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl always performs exactly one locate cycle (hard-coded).

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 1. Set to 0 to skip the locate phase entirely (fast seek only, less accurate). Set to 2+ for higher precision homing at the cost of longer homing time.

codes.name: Homing Cycle 1 Axes
codes.description: Bitmask defining which axes are homed in the first homing cycle. In grblHAL, homing can be configured to home axes in a specific sequence across up to 6 cycles ($44-$49), unlike grbl which uses a fixed Z-first then XY sequence.

examples.description: Home X and Y together in cycle 1

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl uses a fixed homing order: Z first, then X and Y simultaneously. grblHAL allows full customization of homing order and grouping.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Bitmask: X=1, Y=2, Z=4, A=8, B=16, C=32. Axes in the same cycle home simultaneously. Typical CNC setup: $44=4 (Z first), $45=3 (X+Y together). Set to 0 to skip a cycle.

codes.name: Tool Change Mode
codes.description: Configures the tool change behavior when M6 is executed. This is one of the most important grblHAL-specific settings for CNC machines that use multiple tools. Determines whether tool changes are ignored, require manual intervention, or use automatic tool measurement.

examples.description: Normal — M6 pauses for manual tool change (default)
Manual touch-off at home position — moves to home, waits for tool change, probes for length
Manual touch-off at G59.3 — moves to G59.3 position for tool change and probing
Automatic touch-off — changes tool and automatically probes for tool length
Ignore M6 — tool change commands are silently skipped

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support tool changes (M6 is parsed but ignored).
LinuxCNC handles tool changes through HAL and custom remapped M6 procedures. grblHAL's $341 modes provide similar functionality without requiring HAL configuration.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 0. CRITICAL setting for multi-tool CNC work. Mode 0: simple pause. Mode 1: moves to home, user changes tool, then probes at home. Mode 2: moves to G59.3 coordinate (set via G10 L2 P9) for tool change and probing — most common for fixed tool probe setups. Mode 3: fully automatic (requires ATC hardware). Mode 4: ignore M6 (useful for single-tool jobs or testing). Related settings: $342 (probe distance), $343 (slow probe feed), $344 (fast probe feed).

codes.name: Tool Change Probe Distance
codes.description: Sets the maximum distance in mm the tool probe will travel downward when measuring tool length during a tool change. If the probe does not trigger within this distance, an alarm is raised.

examples.description: Probe up to 30mm for tool contact

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support tool change probing.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Used with $341 modes 1, 2, and 3. Set to slightly more than the maximum expected tool stick-out to prevent crashing if no tool is installed or the probe fails.

codes.name: Tool Change Slow Feed Rate
codes.description: Sets the slow (precise) probing feed rate in mm/min used during tool change probing. After the initial fast probe ($344), the tool retracts and re-probes at this slower rate for accurate measurement.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support tool change probing.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Used with $341 modes 1, 2, and 3. Similar concept to $24 (homing locate feed rate) but for tool probing.

codes.name: Tool Change Seek Feed Rate
codes.description: Sets the fast seek feed rate in mm/min used during the initial tool change probe approach. The tool moves down at this rate until first contact, then retracts and re-approaches at the slower $343 rate.

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support tool change probing.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Used with $341 modes 1, 2, and 3. Similar concept to $25 (homing seek rate) but for tool probing.

codes.name: Rotary Axes Bitmask
codes.description: Bitmask identifying which axes are rotary (degrees) rather than linear (mm). Rotary axes use degrees for position, degrees/min for feed rate, and degrees/sec^2 for acceleration. Affects how feed rate is calculated for combined linear+rotary moves.

examples.description: A, B, and C are rotary (bits 3+4+5)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. grbl does not support rotary axes.
LinuxCNC configures rotary axes in the INI file with ANGULAR type.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 0. Bitmask: A=8, B=16, C=32. When an axis is marked rotary, its $1xx steps/mm becomes steps/degree, $1xx max rate becomes degrees/min, and $1xx acceleration becomes degrees/sec^2. For combined linear+rotary moves, feed rate applies to the linear distance only (inverse time mode G93 may be preferred).

codes.name: Disable G92 Persistence
codes.description: Controls whether G92 offsets persist across resets and power cycles. By default (0), G92 offsets are saved to non-volatile storage and restored on startup (matching grbl/NIST behavior). Set to 1 to disable persistence, clearing G92 offsets on every reset.

examples.description: G92 persists across resets (default, matches grbl)
G92 cleared on reset (safer for most workflows)

crossReferences.note: New in grblHAL. In grbl, G92 always persists (no option to disable). This is a common source of confusion when grbl powers up with unexpected offsets.
LinuxCNC also persists G92 by default (stored in .var file) and has DISABLE_G92_PERSISTENCE INI option.

codes.notes: New in grblHAL. Default: 0 (persistent, matching grbl). Many users prefer $384=1 because persistent G92 offsets are a frequent source of confusion — the machine appears to be in the wrong position after a restart because a forgotten G92 offset is still active. Using G54-G59 work coordinates instead of G92 is generally recommended.
